title: ProtocolLab Secure DNS Contract Architecture
purpose: Describe bounded shared DNS wire semantics, transport-specific secure DNS bindings, and classic UDP/TCP diagnostic calibration.
design_summary: The corpus retains dns.plab-test-a.canonical as the cross-transport baseline and adds bounded AAAA, CNAME-chain, NXDOMAIN, NOERROR/NODATA, and 630-byte EDNS/DNSSEC-shaped fixtures. Representative secure semantics stay on selected DoH3 scenarios rather than forming a transport cross-product. One DoH3 GET identity proves RFC 8484 URI-template encoding. Classic UDP/TCP identities are diagnostic only; the large fixture advertises 512 UDP bytes, yields a TC response, and requires an identical-question TCP retry for the full response.
key_components: bounded canonical DNS wire fixture corpus
secure DNS TLS profile and schema
secure DNS and classic calibration profiles and suites
data_and_state_considerations: The target answers the local plab.test. zone authoritatively with recursion desired and recursion available clear, no external upstream, cache disabled, and TTL zero. DoH2, DoH3, and DoQ use message ID zero. DoT uses IDs unique among outstanding queries on a TLS connection and sets the ID to zero before canonical hashing. Responses are parsed and canonical-reserialized before length and hash comparison, so alternate legal compression does not fail raw-wire equality.
edge_cases_and_constraints: DoT, DoQ, and classic TCP include the RFC-required two-octet network-order DNS message length prefix; that transport framing is excluded from the shared bare-message hash.
DoH POST uses the unencoded bare application/dns-message body; DoH GET uses unpadded base64url in the dns query parameter and no request body.
DoT allows pipelining and correlates out-of-order responses by message ID plus question.
DoQ requires one transaction per client-initiated bidirectional stream, FIN in both directions, and RFC 9250 error codes.
Negative fixtures include the authoritative SOA while preserving zero cache TTL.
The DNSSEC-shaped fixture exercises size and record parsing but explicitly does not claim cryptographic signature validity.
UDP truncation-to-TCP retry is diagnostic and is not an allowed secure-transport fallback.
Public recursive resolvers are unsupported comparison targets.
HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 DoH results are separate comparison groups.
DoQ is a DNS binding over QUIC, not raw QUIC transport throughput.
alternatives_considered: Separate DNS payload semantics per transport were rejected because the application message must remain identical.
One generic secure-DNS lane was rejected because the transport bindings are not comparable by default.
risks: Resolver caching can invalidate latency comparisons.
Comparing raw response bytes would reject semantically equivalent compression choices.
HTTP or QUIC connection reuse can hide handshake cost unless requested and effective reuse are recorded.
